Empires & Puzzles Cleaver Hero Review: The Hunting Chieftain Guide
Cleaver was once a fast-speed menace, burying teams in bone traps. But with stats from 2022, is he now the hunter or the hunted? We analyze if his unique mechanics can survive the power creep.
Cleaver Review: Can This Old Hunter Still Trap His Prey?
Cleaver: The "Wet Noodle" Who Refuses to Die

of the Plains Hunter family, arrived in Empires & Puzzles back in August 2022. In an era where hitting for 100% damage feels like a polite tap on the shoulder rather than an attack, Cleaver occupies a strange niche. He is a

speed disruptor who relies entirely on attrition—fiends, bleeding, and attack debuffs—rather than brute force. He doesn't want to one-shot you; he wants to make your life miserable for 15 turns until you eventually lose. Think of him as that one mosquito in your bedroom at 3 AM: he won't kill you, but you won't get any rest.

Visually, Cleaver is fantastic. He sports a rugged, primal aesthetic with a massive bone weapon that looks like it weighs more than the hero himself. The red and earth-tone palette screams "survivalist," and the massive jawbone on his shoulder suggests he's not someone you want to meet during a famine. It’s a shame he hits like he’s swinging a foam replica of that giant axe.
When Cleaver was released, his stats were competitive. In 2026, they are essentially 4-star stats.
Modern attackers regularly exceed 1300 base attack. Cleaver's low attack stat is a double whammy because his Fiends' damage is based on a percentage of his attack.
While 1600 HP was once "tanky," modern heroes sit comfortably at 2200-2400 HP. He is fragile by today's standards unless fully Limit Broken (twice).
"Bone Ritual" is a skill with an identity crisis.
This is negligible. Against modern defenses with 1300+ defense, this will often do double-digit damage. Do not bring him for the impact hit.
This is his main selling point. Summoning a Fiend for

speed is excellent utility. The fiends absorb healing (35% of max health), which is significant against healers, and they deal increasing damage. However, because Cleaver's base attack is low, the fiend damage tickles more than it bites unless you go all-in on attack emblems.

-32% attack to all enemies is a strong survival tool, especially since it scales further down (-5% per hit). This helps mitigate the massive damage output of modern heroes, assuming they don't cleanse it immediately.
Fast (7 tiles). With a lvl 29 mana troop or a bard/bonus, he charges very quickly. This allows him to potentially fire twice before the enemy fires once, stacking his "Hunter's Mark" passive.
100% of 852 Attack is... low. Expect roughly
per hero against high-defense targets. The real damage comes from the Fiends (starting at ~45% attack per turn) and his Passive DoT.
The Fiends absorb 35% of the target's max HP. Against a modern tank with 3000 HP (with troops), that's a
that must be healed off. This is where Cleaver still holds some value.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Plains Hunter family bonus gives an attack boost (up to +20% for 5 heroes). While nice, you are unlikely to run a full team of these older heroes in competitive play today.
A 10% chance to reduce incoming special damage to 1. It’s the "Black Knight" passive but with a much lower trigger rate. When it happens, it feels great; when it doesn't (90% of the time), he dies.
This is a surprisingly effective passive. Dealing 150 damage (stacking up to 1500) adds up over a long fight. Since he is Fast, he can refresh and stack this dot reasonably well on a few targets.

is a decent Aether power for him, as it boosts his special skill damage and attack stat. However, boosting 100% damage is still not going to result in a nuke. It mostly helps his Fiend damage and tile damage.

Upon release, Cleaver was considered "meh" by many due to his low direct damage, but appreciated by high-level players for the fast-speed Fiend spam which crippled healers. He was a staple in Rush Attack and Bloody Battle tournaments.
The game has moved on. We now have heroes who hit for 450% to all while healing or debuffing. Cleaver's mechanical utility (Fast Fiends) is still rare, but his chassis (stats) is rusty.
He is no longer a viable main-team hero for top 100 play. However, he remains useful in
tournaments where his Fiends deal damage that cannot be healed away easily.
Cleaver does not currently have a costume.
Cleaver needs to survive to fire his skill multiple times, but his Fiends rely on his Attack stat. It's a dilemma.
Maximizes Fiend damage (his main source of hurt) and tile damage.
Keeps him alive longer to keep refreshing the Attack Down debuff.
His Fiends become completely ignorable in terms of damage.
. If you are using Cleaver, you are using him for the Fiends. You want those Fiends to actually hurt. If he's just there to survive and do 100 damage, you should bring a different support hero.

He relies on his passives and fiends to do any meaningful work, which takes time—time he often doesn't have.

The "Death by a Thousand Cuts" Team:
Use Cleaver to soften them up and block healing, while other fast heroes pick them apart.
Overwhelm the enemy with so many Fiends that they can never heal again.

Don't pair him with too many other low-damage supports. You need someone to actually kill the enemy.

If you pulled Cleaver early, he is a great asset. His Fast speed will teach you the value of mana control and debuffs. Level him, but think twice before giving him Limit Break materials if you have modern 5-star heroes waiting.

He is likely a "museum piece" for your roster. Unless you are desperate for a Red hero for your 6th War team or love the Monster Hunter aesthetic, he is not worth the Alpha Aethers. There are 4-star heroes released in 2025 (like Toon boldtusk or new Construct heroes) that offer more utility and survivability.
From day one, the community was divided. As one player noted,
, while the other hits like a wet noodle. Who makes these decisions?"
"You know its not the purpose of each and every hero to deal huge amounts of damage? He is a role player to support attacking teams."
Interestingly, despite being considered weak, there was a balance update that tweaked his Fiends, leading to confusion:
"I'm sitting on 30 rings and still just couldn't bring myself to max this guy. Now he's getting a nerf? Hard pass now."
Most players now agree he is
and mostly relegated to specific niche uses or bench warming.

Cleaver is a cool-looking hero with a fun mechanic trapped in an outdated body. He brings a knife (or a bone axe) to a nuclear war. While his Fast Fiends can still annoy unprepared teams in Bloody Battle, he simply lacks the stats and impact to compete in the modern era of Empires & Puzzles. Use him if you love him, but don't expect him to carry you to the top.
A fun toy for the collector, but a risky investment for the competitor.
Is Cleaver still worth your scopes in 2026? A detailed review of the 5-star Fire hero from the Plains Hunter family, analyzing his fiends, stats, and modern viability.
